data.title: Birth of the Cool
data.article:
In 1948, Miles assembled an unusual nonet — nine musicians including a French horn and a tuba — in Gil Evans's basement apartment on West 55th Street. What they created in those cramped quarters would define an entire decade of American music.

The Capitol sessions of 1949–1950 introduced orchestral colour, slower tempos, and a reflective, interior quality that stood in deliberate contrast to bebop's extroversion. The West Coast Cool movement that followed — Dave Brubeck, Chet Baker, Gerry Mulligan — drew directly from this blueprint.

Miles himself moved through a difficult period in the early 1950s, struggling with heroin addiction. He retreated to his father's farm in East St. Louis and emerged clean in 1954. When he returned to the stage, he had something new to say.
